No actual review or information about the dice is present.

To evaluate the dice or the review, the full article or valid content from the source is needed.
Security verification pages are common and do not provide content or claims to fact-check.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the fairness and bias of dice, with references to bugs in game implementations and the physical characteristics of casino dice that affect rolling behavior. Commenters question the adequacy of sample sizes for bias testing and share anecdotes about dice manufacturing and game bugs.

comments.author: rgoldste
comments.text: Reminds me of an encounter on BoardGameArena where the top ranked 7 Wonders Duel player complained there was a randomization bug (the Great Library never offered the science  progress token). I thought he was raging (who hasn’t heard a poker player complain about bad luck) but turns out the developer checked the code and did in fact find this was a bug!
comments.analysis: This comment provides a credible anecdote about a confirmed bug, showing awareness of game mechanics and developer response.

comments.author: the_af
comments.text:
I found this fascinating, if true:

> The reason casino dice have such sharp edges is to get the to stop rolling faster with fewer tumbling. The more a die tumbles the more likely it will present any issues with it.

If I understand it correctly, the justification is this: if a die is biased (usually a heavier face), this bias will manifest with a higher chance the longer the die rolls. But if it stops abruptly, for whatever reason (bumping against the edge of the table, other dice, or having a shape that prevents longer roll time, like the casino dice) this bias will be less likely to manifest. Did I get this explanation right?
comments.analysis: The explanation aligns with known principles of dice physics and casino practices, showing thoughtful interpretation of the dice design.

comments.author: fecal_henge
comments.text: Is a 155 throws enough to evaluate bias? Seems more times than I'd like to roll some dice, but not enough to gain enough measurement confidence. By what criteria is the person assigning the traffic light ratings? What about face coplanarity? Get this enthusiast in a metrology lab.!
comments.analysis: Raises valid concerns about statistical significance and measurement standards, encouraging more rigorous testing.

comments.author: bombcar
comments.text: This reminds me of a D&D dice website that went into way too much detail about how they weren't fair and I remember photos of them stacked on top of each other to show the variations in manufacturing.
comments.analysis: Provides relevant context about dice manufacturing variability, supporting the discussion on dice fairness.

comments.author: riffraff
comments.text: The thread following the review is pretty interesting too!
comments.analysis: This comment adds no substantive information or analysis to the discussion.
